cards.front: What is the recommended IDE for C++ development on Windows?
cards.back: Visual Studio Community (the free version of Visual Studio) is recommended for Windows. It has excellent C++ support and debugging tools.

cards.text: On macOS, the recommended IDE is {{c1::Xcode}}, which is free from the {{c2::Mac App Store}}.

cards.front: What are good cross-platform IDE options for C++?
cards.back: Code::Blocks (free, beginner-friendly) and CLion (commercial, powerful) work on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

cards.front: What is the difference between Visual Studio and Visual Studio Code?
cards.back: Visual Studio is a full IDE with built-in C++ compiler and debugger. Visual Studio Code is a lightweight text editor that requires separate installation and configuration of C++ tools.

cards.text: When installing Visual Studio, make sure to select the {{c1::Desktop development with C++}} workload to get the C++ compiler and tools.

cards.concept: Why beginners should avoid using online compilers for learning
cards.explanation:
Online compilers have limitations:
  • Limited debugging capabilities
  • Can't create multi-file projects easily
  • Unreliable internet dependency
  • Don't teach real-world development workflow
  • Limited customization and settings
A local IDE provides a better learning experience and matches professional development.
examples: Online: limited to single files or simple programs
Local IDE: full debugger with breakpoints and watches
Local IDE: learn real project structure and build systems

cards.front: What IDE options are available for Linux?
cards.back: Code::Blocks, CLion, Qt Creator, or configure a text editor (VS Code, vim, emacs) with C++ tools. Many Linux users prefer command-line tools with makefiles or CMake.

cards.front: What C++ compiler does Visual Studio use?
cards.back: Visual Studio uses MSVC (Microsoft Visual C++ compiler). Xcode uses Clang. GCC is common on Linux.

cards.text: After installing your IDE, verify it works by creating a {{c1::Hello World}} program and successfully {{c2::compiling and running}} it.

cards.front: Why might you want to use the same IDE that the tutorial uses?
cards.back: Using the same IDE makes it easier to follow instructions and screenshots. Different IDEs have different project setup steps and menu layouts.
